guide.html.title: Guide
guide.html.description:
Hi guys, after the first season of hegemony comes to an end, I believe that my alliance and I have gained a lot of information about how hegemony works.So I thought it would be nice to share what we know. Our alliance is called Tenebrae and we ranked 3rd on the overall leaderboard for EU.We did this without having to rely an a 'mega whale' to clearing everything, but instead relied on teamwork and being smart.
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤHegemony is a mode where 8 alliances compete for various structures and crystals in order to accumulate AFK rewards, as well as score points that determine your leaderboard rank.Your alliances initial gameplan should be to scout the opposition and see where they are in relation to your spawn point. If you see a strong clan to the left of your spawn, make sure to spread away from them and go right. The first few days will consist of claiming as many crystals as possible, as well as trying to secure space on the map e.g farming basic castles (again it's good to know your opponents. If you are weaker then prioritize crystal farming. If you are stronger make paths to get castles). You should trial and error with teams that can clear the paths.
It is hard to direct accurately as everyone's power in hegemony varies a lot. For reference, I am 4.7B power and have around 160M power teams at the start of hegemony. I can clear the outside paths with 2 or 3 weak SSR+ heroes.
By the end of the hegemony week I can clear the outside paths with 1 SSR hero. When more of the map opens up, and you are a weaker alliance then you can just keep making paths to get crystals. Crystals become way less of a priority to bigger alliances, especially if 2 big alliances are constantly fighting. Despite this, if you are a weaker alliance, it is not impossible for you to take cities or castles, which i will explain later on.
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤThe fatigue system is very misunderstood, and can be used to MASSIVELY help you in hegemony. The description in game that talks about fatigue doesn't align with what actually happens, thus there can be some confusion about fatigue. I will describe it how I've seen it work in game. When you attack anything in hegemony, your team will gain 5 fatigue stacks (this is the same if you deploy 1 hero to attack, or deploy 6 heroes). After 5, 10, and 15 attacks, your team will gain a 30%, 60%, and 90% stat reduction respectively. Alternatively, if your team is in standby mode at any structure on the map, they can also gain fatigue when getting attacked (the same as described above), however (this part I'm not 100% sure about so correct me if needed) I believe that the attacker must attack with a full 6 man team in order to apply the max stacks of fatigue on a defending team e.g you can't spam a 1 hero team into a defending team to fatigue them. When the defending team gets attacked, then will change from 'standby' mode to 'garrison' mode. The defending teams first defense against an attacking will consume 10 energy. The now garrisoning defense will no longer lose energy if they get attacked again, however they will continue to gain fatigue stacks. To remove the fatigue stacks, you need to leave the base, and re-enter, however this means that if you get attacked again, you will now lose another 10 energy for the initial defense.
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤAfter understanding how the fatigue system works, you can use it to your advantage to capture structures on the map. For this scenario, I will keep it simple and pretend the defending team is AFK, all you need to do is break through. Imagine that there is 1 defending team in the structure that you want to capture, and it has 250M power, but your team is only 100M power. The way to break through is to use the fatigue system to your advantage. Firstly, make your 3 teams full of 6 weak heroes (so 18 weak heroes deployed altogether). Now you will continuously attack the structure. The first 3 attacks will apply 15 fatigue to the defending team. Once you attack the defending team 5 times, it will now have 30% stat reduction. So instead of being a 250M power team, it is technically now at 175M power team. You can keep fatiguing the defense until it is sufficient to defeat with your main team. This strategy loses a lot of energy for your weak heroes, however allows you to overcome the strong opposition. This strategy also doesn't rely on anybody else's help from your alliance, so it is good to know if you are in an inactive alliance.
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤFor this scenario, I will continue from the above scenario. You managed to capture the structure! However the enemy just woke up and is determined to claim it back. If you leave you team in the structure to defend, they will just one shot it, then proceed to capture the structure. So how can you defend it by yourself? Again, take advantage of the fatigue system. Move your main team 1 tile outside of the structure, preferably directly opposite from where they are attacking from. After the attacking team starts attacking the structure, they will start to gain fatigue stacks. So now wait until they have completed either 5, 10, or 15 battles (whatever amount of fatigue is needed for you to defeat them) and then move your defense back into the structure and your team will prevail! 1 of 2 things will happen now : They give up
OR
They keep full sending it Remember though, this one defense cost your team 10 energy, and put them into 'garrison' mode. To continue to defend, you need to move out, then into the base to heal up, then back out of the base and wait for the attacker to attack.
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤThis information will be aimed for stronger alliances (relative to opposition) that are fighting other strong alliances.
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤMaking use of the legion march feature is one of the most important things for hegemony, HOWEVER it requires an active and competent alliance leader, and requires some coordination with other guild members. Anybody from your alliance has the ability to create a legion, however you need to be assigned as a city lord of a particular city or castle. Only your alliance leader can assign you to be a city lord, thus the leader himself need to be active. Once you are a city lord, you need alliance members to find the city/castle in which you are city lord, click on the structure, and press 'proceed to assignment' This will move their teams to your structure, and allow you to form a 'legion conscription'. You can only select a maximum of 20 teams to join the conscription, however more than 20 teams can be left 'as assignment' in your structure. Select the 20 (or however many amount of teams you desire) teams and create your legion. Useful information: if there is currently a path to a specific structure you want to attack, you will be able to start your legion march. If during the march the path gets broken, the legion will still continue marching and stop at the tile where the path is broken. The legion is only able to attack structures on the map, excluding crystals. You cannot use a legion to form paths for you. I am currently unsure as to how to determine the attack order of your legion, but i can confirm that it is not based from a power scale (at least not from what I've seen) when the legion attacks a structure, all teams attack at once, so if you have 20 teams, you will take out 20 hp from a structure at a time, which allows you to very quickly capture important structures. Sometimes speed is more useful then energy efficiency.
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤ
ㅤThe final 2 hours can get a bit hectic if you are against other strong alliances that also want to win. If your team is already winning based on score, then you actually don't need to do anything. You can apply the defending tactic from above to defend your main structures e.g. the lions, swift stele, adv cities etc. You do NOT want to attack the genesis idol and 'help' the opposition claim it. Instead, the first course of action will be controlling space, and disallowing the enemies to get close to the genesis idol. If you have a legion available, create a legion march to one of the lions and wait there. You can't wait 1 tile away from the idol as you can only march to structures, not tiles/crystals. Inevitably, a breakthrough may happen and they will start attacking the idol. If they do, be prepared to wait outside until the idol is low hp. If you have a legion close by, and the idol has 20 hp, your legion can be sent into the idol to guarantee the last hit capture. Defense strategies from earlier apply here again to defend your lead.

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_showFrontmatterTags.description: Show tags defined in the frontmatter of the document at the top of the page.

info_showCustomIcons.description: Show custom icons for files and folders

info_showDefaultFolderIcons.description: Show a default icon of a folder for every folder in the tree

info_showDefaultFileIcons.description: Show a default icon of a file for every file in the tree

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_exposeStartingPath.description: Whether or not to show the current file in the file tree when the page is first loaded

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_minCollapseDepth.description: Only allow outline items to be collapsed if they are at least this many levels deep in the tree.

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
